coredata.dc:title: Metal free, sunlight and white light based photocatalysis using carbon quantum dots from Citrus grandis: A green way to remove pollution
coredata.dc:creator: Ramar, Venkadeshkumar
Moothattu, Shaheer
Balasubramanian, Karthikeyan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have developed a facile and green synthesis of Undoped carbon quantum dots (UCQDs) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) from naturally available Pomelo (Citrus maxima or Citrus grandis) juice as a precursor using one-step hydrothermal method. As synthesized CQDs were characterized by X Ray Diffraction (XRD), UV–Visible spectroscopy, Photoluminescence (PL), Raman Spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) technique and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. Moreover, we have demonstrated that both the prepared CQDs could serve as an excellent photocatalyst towards the degradation of the Methylene Blue (MB) dye under direct sun light radiation. The degradation mechanism of MB dye could be approximated as pseudo - first order kinetics according to the Langmuir – Hinshelwood model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalysis
Solar light
Methylene blue
Degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Pomelo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern for (a) UCQDs and (b) NCQDs.

Excitation-dependent PL spectra recorded in an aqueous dispersion of CQDs (a) UCQD, (b) NCQD.
The FT-IR spectra of UCQDs and NCQDs.

UV–Vis spectrum for the De-coloration of MB dye (a) and (b) under white light irradiation with UCQDs and NCQDs respectively. (c) And (d) under the sun light irradiation with UCQDs and NCQDs respectively.
Mechanism of the electron transfer due to solar energy and decoloration of MB dye.

De-coloration rate of MB dye. (a) And (b) shows
C /
C
o
and Ln
( C /
C
o
)
plots of CQDs under white light illumination. (c) And (d) shows
C /
C
o
and Ln
( C /
C
o
)
plots of CQDs under the sun light illumination.

head.ce:title: Metal free, sunlight and white light based photocatalysis using carbon quantum dots from Citrus grandis: A green way to remove pollution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanophotonics Laboratory, Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Tiruchirappalli 620015, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have developed a facile and green synthesis of Undoped carbon quantum dots (UCQDs) and N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) from naturally available Pomelo (Citrus maxima or Citrus grandis) juice as a precursor using one-step hydrothermal method. As synthesized CQDs were characterized by X Ray Diffraction (XRD), UV–Visible spectroscopy, Photoluminescence (PL), Raman Spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) technique and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques. Moreover, we have demonstrated that both the prepared CQDs could serve as an excellent photocatalyst towards the degradation of the Methylene Blue (MB) dye under direct sun light radiation. The degradation mechanism of MB dye could be approximated as pseudo - first order kinetics according to the Langmuir – Hinshelwood model.

ce:section.ce:para: As of late, scientists are demonstrating incredible enthusiasm on manufacturing carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) on account of its extensive variety of utilization in the fields of Bioimaging (Sahu et al., 2012), optoelectronics (Li et al., 2015), Photocatalysis (Li et al., 2010), Drug conveyance (Qi and Gao, 2008) and natural sensor (Wang and Hu, 2014). From the last decade, specialist have shown more interest in making the CQDs from the natural sources like Carbohydrate (Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009), Orange juice (Kumar et al., 2014), Lime (Barati et al., 2015), Ginger (Li et al., 2014), Flower (Feng et al., 2015), Cabbage (Alam et al., 2015), Tamarind (Jhonsi and Thulasi, 2016), Sugarcane Bagasse (Thambiraj and Shankaran, 2016), Honey (Mandani et al., 2017), Papaya juice (Kasibabu et al., 2015), Banana juice (De and Karak, 2013), Soya beans (Li et al., 2013) and so on. Especially the CQDs extracted from such sources have magnificent properties like compound idleness, improved optical properties, and so on. Recently Lu et al., prepared the CQDs from Pomelo peel (Lu et al., 2012) for the sensing application. In this work we prepared the CQDs using Pomelo juice. To prepare the CQDs, the available methodologies are Chemical ablation (Qiao et al., 2010; Ray et al., 2009), Laser ablation (Yogesh et al., 2017), Electrochemical carbonization (Ming et al., 2012; Zheng et al., 2009), Microwave irradiation (Liu et al., 2014; Zhai et al., 2012), Hydrothermal/Solvothermal treatment (Alam et al., 2015; De and Karak, 2013; Jhonsi and Thulasi, 2016). Among this technique, we have chosen a hydrothermal method for synthesis of CQDs. Hydrothermal method is considered to be an easy, shortest and well-organized among the reported techniques.

The photocatalysis thought was initially proposed by Ciamician (Ciamician, 1912). However, the most acknowledged term photocatalysis and noteworthy advancements in this field appropriately began in the 1970s after the disclosure of water photolysis on a TiO2 cathode by Fujishima and Honda (1972). The photocatalysis story began with solar power translation and then shifted to environmental photocatalysis and most recently this shifted to photo-induced hydrophilicity. Solar light energy is the principle driver of the entire biological and environmental processes and it is the naturally available source. We need to harvest and utilize the naturally available sunlight energy (Wang et al., 2016). So, in this work, we used a lightsource as a white light and solar light energy. Sunlight based photocatalysis are extensively studied over the past four decades but depends on the catalyst still being actively investigated as a core technology (Antil-Martini et al., 2017; Markad et al., 2017; Natarajan et al., 2017). The wide light-assimilation property of CQDs has motivated their application in photocatalysis. The photocatalytic properties of the CQDs are similarly less contemplated in the past couple of years. A variety of semiconductor photocatalyst (TiO2 (Fujishima and Honda, 1972), C3N4 (Pan et al., 2012), Fe2O3 (Jang et al., 2009), Cu2O (Pandiyarajan et al., 2016), Bi2WO6 (Zhang et al., 2012), Bi2MoO6 (Wang et al., 2016), BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I)) with different morphologies has been used in the photocatalysis field for improving the photocatalytic activity. The modification of the surface structure of the photocatalyst significantly influences on the whole photocatalytic mechanism and kinetics. CQDs have been attractive and drawn more attention in the photocatalysis field. One of the essential aspects of the environmental photocatalyst is the availability of the material. CQDs naturally exist in a variety of arrangements (Alam et al., 2015; Barati et al., 2015; De and Karak, 2013; Feng et al., 2015; Jhonsi and Thulasi, 2016; Kasibabu et al., 2015; Kumar et al., 2014; Li et al., 2013, 2014; Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009; Thambiraj and Shankaran, 2016; Yang et al., 2014). The ideal photocatalyst should have the properties such as comparatively inexpensive, chemically stable, water soluble and the photo-induced holes to be an oxidizing agent, as well as the photo-induced electrons, are able to produce superoxide anions from dioxygen. In addition, an ideal photocatalyst should possess both a wide photoabsorption range and a high separation efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Pomelo
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment from naturally acquired Pomelo (Citrus maxima or Citrus grandis). Fresh Pomelo fruit was purchased from a local market in Tiruchirappalli, India. After its skin was peeled, the Pomelo was crushed in a mortar. The extracted Pomelo juice was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min. The obtained supernatant was passed through a filter paper with slow filtration speed. Afterwards, the filtrate juice was transferred into 100 ml Teflon lined stainless steel laboratory autoclave and heated in a furnace with a constant temperature of 200 °C for a period of 7 h. During the process of hydrothermal, the pomelo undergoes dehydration, polymerization and carbonization occurred among the organic molecules of protein, oligosaccharide, and cellulose remained in Pomelo. A dark brown product was obtained after cooling to room temperature. After that, the aqueous solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 30 min to acquire CQDs. The obtained CQDs were used for further usage. The same procedure was carried out for preparation of the N-doped carbon quantum dots, but before pouring the prepared juice into the Teflon we have added Ammonium bicarbonate.The proposed mechanism for the formation of CQDs shown in Fig. 1 .

XRD pattern of the prepared samples were observed using Rigaku diffractometer (Ultima III, Japan) and this is equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) with the operating voltage of 40 kV and a beam current of 30 mA. The optical absorption study was done by using JASCO V-630 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence studies were done in a Fluoromax-P-Horiba JobinYvon Luminescence spectroflurometer. The functional groups were determined by Perkin Elmer Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer. The Raman spectral measurements were carried out using LabRAM HR Evolution Raman Spectrometer having an excitation wavelength of 532 nm laser source with a laser power of 50 mW. The morphology and size of the prepared CQDs are characterized by High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM JEOL-2200).

The XRD pattern of the prepared UCQD’s and NCQD’s are shown in Fig. 2 . In Fig. 2(a) an intense diffraction peak arises around 22.4° (d = 0.396 nm) due to amorphous nature of the UCQDs. Similarly in Fig. 2(b) there are two strong diffraction peaks observed around 12.6° (d = 0.7 nm), 28.2° (d = 0.316 nm) which clearly indicates that the prepared NCQDs have amorphous structure.

TEM results revealed the average particle size of the UCQD’s in Fig. 3 a is 3 nm by the statistical analysis over fifty particles. Fig. 3c shows the size distribution of the UCQDs particles in histogram plot which is well agreed with the Gaussian distribution. Fig. 3b indicates the TEM results for the NCQD’s with the average particle size of 70 nm. Fig. 3d represents the particle size distribution with Gaussian fitting.

The UV absorbance curve of the prepared UCQD’s and NCQD’s are shown in the Fig. 4 . UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the UCQDs exhibit two strong absorption bands, which can be seen as a typical absorption of an aromatic system. The first absorption band was observed at 225 nm, which is attributed to the n-π∗ transition with CN bonds. The second typical absorption peak was observed at 284 nm, which is attributed to the π-π∗ transition with CC bonds for aromatic sp2 hybridisation (Meiling et al., 2016). The absorption spectra of the NCQD displays a strong peak at 270 nm which is ascribed to the transition from n-π∗with CO bonds. The absorption band at 300 nm indicates that the prepared NCQD molecules having amide functions (Ji et al., 2016).

The photoluminescence spectra for UCQD’s and NCQD’s are shown in Fig. 5 . This shows the excitation-dependent emission spectral behavior of CQDs. In Fig. 5a when the excitation wavelength varied from 330 to 470 nm with the increment of 20 nm, it is observed that emission varies from 420 to 510 nm. The strong emission peak was located at 452 nm with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm for UCQD’s. Similarly NCQD’s in Fig. 5b shows the strong emission peak at 447 nm with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. When the excitation wavelength was higher than 370 nm, the emission intensity of both CQD’s decreased remarkably and exhibited red shift with the excitation to the longer wavelength. The conceivable explanation behind the PL behavior is due to the presence of distinctive particle sizes and the distribution of the different surface energy traps of the CQD’s. Consequently, the particle size is smaller as it gets excited at lower wavelength and the particle size is larger as it gets excited at higher wavelength. The PL emission intensity depends on the number of particles excited by the particular excitation wavelength.

The highest PL emission intensity of CQDs was observed at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, due to a large number of particles are excited at this particular excitation wavelength. The multi-PL emission sites are produced with the change of surface properties and they could be in charge of excitation-dependent PL behavior of CQD’s.

We also recorded the FT-IR spectrum, to study the presence of functional groups and surface structure of the CQD’s as shown in Fig. 6 . The broad absorption band located at the 3300 cm−1 are assigned to compound with hydroxyl group O-H stretching vibration which represents the huge number of outstanding hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CQD’s (Kasibabu et al., 2015; Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009). The characteristic band at 1637 cm−1 are ascribed to CO bending vibration which shows the presence of carboxylgroup (Alam et al., 2015). Furthermore, the sharp peak at 1637 cm−1 suggests the formation of CO vibrational bending confirms the result of UV–Vis absorbance at 284 nm ascribed to the π-π∗ transition of aromatic CQD’s. Therefore, the CQD’s are having a carboxyl group (CO) and a hydroxyl group (OH). This shows the excellent water solubility property of the synthesized CQDs without further chemical modification. NCQD’s also shows the same bands with no shift.

Raman spectroscopy is a quick, suitable and powerful non-destructive technique for the identification and analysis of nanostructures. The recorded Raman spectra with a Lorentzian fit for the both UCQD’s and NCQD’s are shown in Fig. 7 . In this Fig. 7a UCQD’s exhibit two Raman bands. One sharp intense band observed at 1571 cm−1, this band is known as G-Band and another feeble band observed at 1354 cm−1 this is known D-Band. The D band arises due to the living mode of κ-point phonons of A1g equilibrium and sp2 hybridized carbon domains. The G band arises due to the presence of isolated double bonds and comes from the first order scattering of E2g phonon of sp2 hybridized C atoms. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was calculated as 0.86 which represents the cleanliness of the UCQD’s and this intensity ratio is inversely proportional to the common size of the sp2 crystalline area. Similarly, in the NCQD’s Raman spectrum Fig. 7b shows a D-band at 1335 cm−1 which attributes to the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon systems and G-band at 1563 cm−1 which confirms the stretching of CC bonds in NCQD’s. This G-band is highly sensitive to the strain effects in sp2 system. The intensity ratio of the ID/IG was calculated as 0.854 which is almost similar to the UCQD’s.

But under the similar synthesis method the D-band is shifted down from 1354 to 1335 cm−1 and the G-band is shifted down from 1571 to 1563 cm−1. This red shift is due to the doping of Nitrogen (Wang et al., 2015).

The evaluation of photocatalytic activity of the prepared CQD’s was demonstrated by monitoring the MB dye (IUPAC name is 3,7-bis (Dimethylamino)-phenothiazine-5-ium chloride) de-coloration in an aqueous solution in the presence of White light and Sunlight. We have prepared 10 µM Methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S, 3H2O) solutions in 100 ml of water. Then we added the CQD’s to the prepared MB dye solution. Subsequently, the mixture was kept in the constant stirring with the exposure of white light using compact fluorescent lamp (CFL) with 20 W. Before the exposure of light to the above mixture, the light was allowed to glow 20 min to attain the maximum output power and entire experiment was carried out under the darkened condition. The light source was allowed to expose for 90 min and the samples were collected at every 10 min interval of time. The same solution was kept under the direct sunlight in the afternoon time (12–2 PM). The same were followed for the sunlight photocatalysis. Then the de-coloration efficiency of MB was analyzed using UV–Vis spectrometer. The Fig. 8 shows the de-coloration of MB, when treated with prepared CQD’s in the white light and sunlight irradiation with different time interval. The peaks were observed at 613 and 664 nm. The de-coloration percentage can calculate using the below formula (Ramli et al., 2014),
(1)
R =
(
C
0
-
C
t
)
C
0
× 100
where C0 is the initial concentration of MB solution and Ct is the concentration of MB solution during the irradiation.

In colloidal TiO2 and other nanoscale semiconductors (C3N4, TiO2, Fe2O3, Cu2O, Bi2WO6, Bi2MoO6, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I)) for the photocatalytic conversion, which concentrates the photo-generated electrons against their recombination with the holes, could improve the photocatalytic conversion significantly. The same approach was applied to the CQD’s as photocatalysts. An attractive observation in the use of CQD’s as a photocatalyst is CO2 reduction. At higher concentration of CQD’s, the product yield reliance of CO2 reduction is superior.

Photocatalytic activity significantly depends on the transfer the photo-generated electrons and holes as well as departure efficiencies of photo-generated electrons and holes in the photocatalysts. Encouraging the separation of photo induced electrons and holes will make the photo catalytic process easier. Moreover, photo-generated CQD’s are both outstanding electron donors and acceptors, resulting in incompetent separation of electrons and holes. The significant photo-induced electron transfer property of CQD’s opens up new opportunities for their potential uses in redox process like light energy conversion and related applications (Wang et al., 2017).

Fig. 8(a) and (b) shows the insignificant change in the solution after a long time illumination of white light which is indicating that the MB molecule with UCQDs and NCQDs is highly stable in white light. But in the case of sun light illumination Fig. 8(c) and (d) the characteristic absorption band of MB dye at 613 and 664 nm was gradually decreased as a function of sun light irradiation time. This is suggesting that the solar light can have the ability to make the CQDs as catalyst. The mechanism of de-coloration of MB dye process is shown in Fig. 9 and it is explained as follows: CQDs act as catalyst and light absorber. The MB dye will be adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst (CQDs). Then the sun light energy is supplied to the system. Due to this external sun light energy the valance band electrons (
e
cb
-
) are excited to the conduction band of the CQDs (Sharma et al., 2015). Because of the electron transfer a hole will be created in the valance band.

The photo-induced electrons scavenge the molecular oxygen which is adsorbed on the surface of the CQDs to produce superoxide radical anions
O
2
-
and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Konstantinou and Albanis, 2004).
(2) CQDs +hν(sun light)→CQD (e cb - +hvb +)
(3) O2 +ecb - →O2 -
(4) 2H+ +O2 +2e- →H2O2

The photo-generated hole (h+) react with OH − or H2O oxidizing them into OH∗ radical (Konstantinou and Albanis, 2004).
(5) H2O+hvb + →OH∗ +H+
(6) H2O2 +O2 - →OH∗ +O2 +OH-
(7) MB (C16 H18 ClN3S, 3H2O)+hvb + →Oxidation
(8) MB (C16H18ClN3S, 3H2O)+ecb - →Reduction
(9) MB (C16H18ClN3S, 3H2O)+OH∗ →Degradation

The OH∗ radicals are very strong oxidizing agent and they can de-colorize most of the MB dye. The superoxide radical anions
(
O
2
-
)
and hole produced by electron transfer
(
h
vb
+
) will contribute to the de-coloration of the MB dye as follows Sharma et al. (2015),
(10) MB (C16H18ClN3S, 3H2O)+O2 - →CO2 +H2O
(11) MB (C16H18ClN3S, 3H2O)+hvb + →CO2 +H2O

This experiment suggested that both photo-oxidation and photosensitizing mechanism occurred during the irradiation of the sunlight on the CQDs. The sunlight source is essential for the reaction to occur.

In the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model (L-H model), the rate of reaction is directly proportional to the surface coverage
Θ
. (where
Θ
= KC/(1 + KC)) (Galindo et al., 2000; Tang and An, 1995). Therefore, the rate of product formation can be written as,
(12)
r =
dC
dt
=
kKC
( 1
+
KC )
where k is the reaction rate constant (M−1s−1) and K is the adsorption constant (mol−1) and C is the concentration of the dye at the time‘t’.

The photo catalytic degradation of MB under the sunlight irradiation obeyed the pseudo – first order kinetics is expressed by the following equation,
(13)
Ln
( C /
C
o
) = kKt
(OR)
(14)
( C /
C
o
) =
e
- kKt

where
C
o
and
C
are the concentration of MB dye at the irradiation time 0 and t. A plot of
C /
C
o
versus time represents the de-coloration rate of MB dye with respect to time. Fig. 10 c shows the error analysis
C /
C
o
. The error analysis were calculated using the following standard deviation formula,
(15)
σ
2
=
lim
n → ∞
1
N
∑
(
x
i
- μ )
2

where
σ
the standard deviation, x is the observed values and
μ
is the mean value. Standard deviation
σ
indicates that the uncertainties associated with our experimental attempts. Usually we cannot know the true value and can only estimate the errors essential in the research. If we repeat the trial, the results may well differ from those of the first attempt. It is important to distinguish between precision and accuracy. The precision is a measurement of reproducibility of the result in a given experiment. The accuracy of an experiment is a measure of how close the result of the experiment is to the true value (Bevington et al., 1993). The distinction between the accuracy and precision illustrated in Fig. 10c where the scatter of the data points is a reflection of uncertainties in the measurements, consistent with the error bars on the points. The error term suggest a deviation of the result from the true value. Although in general the distribution resulting from purely statistical errors can be well defined by the two factors mean and standard deviation, we should be aware that, at distances of a few standard deviations from the mean of an experimental distribution, non statistical errors may dominate. From the Fig. 10c it is observed that the degradation efficiency of NCQDs is little bit higher than the UCQDs.

In summary, even though several methods have been proposed towards the synthesis of CQDs, it is critical to synthesize CQDs in a facile and green manner. This is the first time we have established a facile one step synthesis of CQDs from Pomelo juice using hydrothermal method. As synthesized CQDs have excellent solar light harvesting and the unique photo generated electron transfer property, which makes the CQDs as the outstanding photocatalytic applications. CQDs exhibit an excellent de-coloration of organic dyes under the solar light irradiation. Compared to the previous reported synthesis and application, two features become noticeable. One is our starting material for the preparation of CQDs is naturally available Pomelo fruit and it is a green method and the second one is to induce the electrons from valance band to the conduction band, the light source used is a naturally available solar light source.
